Curriculum → Case studies and interview drills → Case study: URL shortener

Lesson 1 of 9

Case study: URL shortener

Design a service that creates short codes, resolves them quickly, tracks analytics, and avoids hot-key failures.

25-35 min Case studies and interview drills 4 guided stops

Study map

What this lesson helps you explain

This case is deceptively small and is excellent practice for storage design, cache placement, and throughput reasoning.

Start here Frame the trade-off before diving into architecture. 1. Core flow Short-code generation can be random, counter-based, or hash derived2. Scaling decisions Cache hot mappings near the edge or in Redis3. Operational nuances Validate target URLs and protect against malicious redirects

Guided walkthrough

Move through the lesson in a clear interview order

4 steps

Start with the framing, walk the concept in order, then use the right rail for prompts, pitfalls, and visual anchors.

1. Interview framing This case is deceptively small and is excellent practice for storage design, cache placement, and throughput reasoning. 2. Core flow Short-code generation can be random, counter-based, or hash derived3. Scaling decisions Cache hot mappings near the edge or in Redis4. Operational nuances Validate target URLs and protect against malicious redirects
1

Start with this framing

Interview framing

This case is deceptively small and is excellent practice for storage design, cache placement, and throughput reasoning.

What to say first

  • Explain short-code generation and collision handling.
  • Optimize the hot read path with caching.
  • Move analytics off the critical redirect path.
2

Study section

Core flow

Write path creates a short code, stores the mapping, and optionally records metadata. Read path resolves the code with very low latency.

Call these points out explicitly

  • Short-code generation can be random, counter-based, or hash derived
  • Collision handling and reserved words must be addressed
  • Analytics often belongs in an async pipeline
3

Study section

Scaling decisions

Read traffic is usually much higher than writes, so aggressive caching and CDN-friendly redirects are natural optimizations.

Call these points out explicitly

  • Cache hot mappings near the edge or in Redis
  • Partition by short code or hash prefix
  • Use append-only analytics logs instead of synchronous counters
4

Study section

Operational nuances

Custom aliases, expiration, abuse detection, and safe redirect behavior quickly turn a toy problem into a production design.

Call these points out explicitly

  • Validate target URLs and protect against malicious redirects
  • Support expiration and deletion workflows
  • Rate-limit creation APIs to reduce abuse

Reference diagram

Visual anchor

A URL shortener is a compact way to practice hot reads, mapping storage, and async analytics.

Adapted from The System Design Primer by Donne Martin (CC BY 4.0).

Checklist

Things to say clearly

  • Explain short-code generation and collision handling.
  • Optimize the hot read path with caching.
  • Move analytics off the critical redirect path.
  • Mention abuse prevention and TTL handling.

Common pitfalls

What to avoid

  • Using a global counter without discussing bottlenecks or availability.
  • Updating analytics synchronously on every redirect.
  • Ignoring malicious destination URLs or spam.

Interview prompts

Practice aloud

  • How would you generate unique short codes at high write volume?
  • What would you cache for fast redirects?
  • How do analytics change the design?

Related topics

Keep the thread going

Back-of-the-envelope estimation Foundations and estimationCaching layers and cache placement Caching, asynchrony, and resilience

Interactive topic lab

URL shortener architecture studio

This case study should feel like a guided interview. Explore the redirect path, compare code-generation strategies, and see how different storage choices change latency, abuse handling, and operational risk.

Optimize the redirect path first because reads dominate.Keep analytics and abuse enrichment off the critical path.Choose a code-generation strategy based on collision risk, availability, and operational simplicity.

How to explain it

High-signal talking points

  • Optimize the redirect path first because reads dominate.
  • Keep analytics and abuse enrichment off the critical path.
  • Choose a code-generation strategy based on collision risk, availability, and operational simplicity.

Interactive diagram

Shortener critical paths

Rendering diagram…

Separate the hot redirect path from slower analytics and abuse-processing workflows.

Worked example

A few links become globally hot while writes stay modest

A marketing platform creates only thousands of links per hour, but campaign launches create huge spikes on a handful of redirect keys.

Recommended choice

Store canonical mappings in a durable primary and push hot redirects into Redis or edge caches.

Why it fits

  • The hot path becomes a memory lookup plus redirect response.
  • Negative caching and request collapsing protect the database during misses.
  • Analytics stays asynchronous so read latency stays tiny under spikes.

If you chose differently

If every redirect increments counters synchronously in the primary database, a single hot campaign turns analytics into a latency and availability problem.

Interview-ready takeaway

The best explanation is “fast redirect path, slow analytics path,” with explicit hot-key protection.

Simulation lab

Simulation lab: URL shortener under load

Edit the topology, switch API workloads, inject failures, and see how redirect latency, write pressure, and queue lag evolve before you propose fixes.

A system design answer gets stronger when you can connect traffic shape to concrete bottlenecks.Hot redirect traffic and asynchronous analytics stress a URL shortener in very different ways.The right fix depends on which node saturates first, not on the fanciest architecture.

Choose an API path

Which flow are you stressing?

Focus metrics

  • redirect p95
  • cache hit ratio
  • database utilization
  • analytics queue depth

Workload profile

Traffic shape

Exercise workspace

Multi-file topology and override editor

6 nodes

Use file tabs, code completion, inline previews, and diagnostics to tune the topology and scripted overrides without leaving the browser.

topology.flow Edit the topology and override script with quick inserts, inline diagnostics, and visible structure signals.
11 lines · 71 words
6 nodes · 5 links
Browser · Native flow-graph compilerWASM · Pyodide / Python WASM adapterWASM · Lua / custom WASM adapter

Snapshot

Current file summary

6 nodes · 5 links

Signals

What the editor found

  • Line 1: edge · 60000 rps · 4 ms
  • Line 2: service · 22000 rps · 12 ms
  • Line 3: cache · 85000 rps · 2 ms
  • Line 4: database · 7500 rps · 18 ms
  • Line 5: queue · 30000 rps · 10 ms
  • Line 6: worker · 20000 rps · 22 ms

Built-in runtime adapters

Browser and WebAssembly execution targets

No eval

The simulator runs its DSL interpreters in-browser today and now exposes adapter slots for WebAssembly-backed runtimes like Pyodide or custom interpreters.

Native flow-graph compiler

Runs the topology and simulation DSL interpreters directly in the browser.

Available now
Pyodide / Python WASM adapter

Ready for browser-side Python interpreters compiled to WebAssembly.

Register adapter
Lua / custom WASM adapter

Use this slot for additional in-browser interpreters backed by WebAssembly modules.

Register adapter

Good first experiments

  • Protect the hot redirect path before optimizing secondary analytics.
  • Say what should stay synchronous and what should move behind queues.
  • Choose fixes that match the measured bottleneck: cache, replica, queue, shard, or rate limit.

Interactive diagram

Compiled simulation topology

Rendering diagram…

This preview is generated from the current flow-graph input.

API modeling notes

How this simulator thinks

  • The redirect API reads hot keys from cache and only hits the database on cache miss.
  • The create API is write-heavy and should keep collision checks and persistence on the colder path.
  • Analytics should stay asynchronous so a hot campaign does not make every click slower.

What you get after a run

Simulation output

  • Per-node utilization, queue depth, and dropped traffic
  • Average, p95, and p99 latency for the selected API path
  • Probable fixes mapped to the dominant bottleneck

Answers stay in your browser so you can come back later without needing automated grading.

What you will practice

design

Interview opening

Not saved yet

You are starting a mock interview for "Case study: URL shortener". Capture the product assumptions, scale estimates, core APIs, and the first diagram you would draw.

Goal for this step

Frame the problem, scope, assumptions, and success metrics before you draw components.

Use these guardrails

  • How would you generate unique short codes at high write volume?
  • Core flow
  • Scaling decisions
  • Explain short-code generation and collision handling.

Suggested structure

  • Requirements and assumptions
  • Scale estimate
  • Core APIs
  • First diagram
